claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 7 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 7 years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have a 7-year-old son named Alex.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few questions:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to understand your relationship with Alex:

**Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to ask:

**Do you make financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or towards his accommodation)?** If yes, approximately how much do you contribute and how often?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards his upkeep. I help with things like his clothes, school expenses, and other necessities. I give £50 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know you're contributing £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep.

Now I need to understand the living arrangements better:

**Who does Alex currently live with? Is it his other parent, another relative, or someone else?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's in local authority care - he's been in a care home for a few months now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I understand Alex has been in local authority care for a few months.

This is an important detail for Child Benefit eligibility. I need to ask:

**Does Alex spend any regular time at home with you - for example, does he come home for at least 24 hours per week, or does he spend at least 2 nights in a row each week at home with you, or 7 or more days in a row at home?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't come home to stay with me. He's at the care home full-time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me, Alex has been in local authority care for a few months (which is more than 8 weeks), and he doesn't spend regular time at home with you (such as 24 hours per week or overnight stays).

According to the Child Benefit rules, when a child goes into local authority care for more than 8 weeks, you're usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit. There is an exception if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home, but since Alex is in the care home full-time and doesn't come home to stay with you, this exception doesn't apply in your situation.

While it's positive that you're contributing £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, the rules for children in care are different from situations where a child simply lives with someone else. The key factor is that the local authority is responsible for Alex's care and accommodation.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

I'm sorry to deliver this news. If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if Alex starts spending regular time at home with you (at least 24 hours per week) - you may be able to claim Child Benefit at that point. In that case, you would need to contact HMRC to discuss your situation.

If you have any questions about this decision or if your circumstances change, you can contact the Child Benefit Office for further guidance.

reasonings: Alex is 7 years old and the claimant lives in the UK, which meets the basic age and residency requirements. However, Alex has been in local authority care for a few months (more than 8 weeks) and does not live with the claimant. According to the rules, when a child goes into care for more than 8 weeks, Child Benefit entitlement usually ends. There is an exception if the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home, but Alex is in the care home full-time and does not come home to stay with the claimant. Although the claimant contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, this does not change the eligibility outcome for children in local authority care, as the rules differ from situations where a child simply lives with someone else. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 7), who has been in local authority care for several months. While the claimant lives in the UK and contributes financially to Alex's upkeep, Child Benefit is not payable when a child has been in care for more than 8 weeks unless they spend at least 24 hours per week at home. Since Alex is in the care home full-time with no regular home visits, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.

While the claimant lives in the UK and contributes financially to Alex's upkeep, Child Benefit is not payable when a child has been in care for more than 8 weeks unless they spend at least 24 hours per week at home. Since Alex is in the care home full-time with no regular home visits, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
